agenda_briefs.title: Council to consider 2025 Homeless Count results for Vancouver
agenda_briefs.summary: Vancouver City Council will receive the 2025 Homeless Count results, which identified 2,715 homeless individuals on March 11, 2025 (1,952 sheltered, 763 unsheltered), a 12% increase from 2023. The count shows 79% of respondents have been homeless for at least a year, and Indigenous peoples are 18 times more likely to be homeless than non-Indigenous. This is an information item only — no bylaw or regulation is being voted on.
agenda_briefs.source_body:
1.	2025 Homeless Count – Vancouver
(no speakers per section 7.2(d) of the Procedure By-law)
Report

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: Burnaby 2026 Cultural Grants for Operating & Spaces
agenda_briefs.summary: Council will vote on $5,509,770 in grants to 85 cultural organizations, including $2,828,160 for five institutions, $709,760 for 26 annual operating grants, and $1,897,550 per year for three years (2026–2028) for 52 multi-year grantees. Additional grants include $75,000 for the Vancouver Concert Hall and Theatre Society's Phase II feasibility study and $728,000 for hazardous materials abatement and accessibility upgrades at Historic Joy Kogawa House. Recommendations A–C, E, and G require a two-thirds majority vote under Section 206(1) of the Vancouver Charter.
agenda_briefs.action_required: If you run a cultural organization, watch for the council vote on April 14, 2026 — approved grants would fund operations, spaces, or capital projects.
agenda_briefs.source_body:

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: Vancouver Community Sport Event Grants for Spring 2026
agenda_briefs.summary: Council is set to vote on awarding 19 grants totalling $89,000 to community sport events in Vancouver during Spring 2026. The grants range from $1,000 to $5,000 per event, with events scheduled from May 1 to August 30, 2026. The recommendation requires at least two-thirds of all council members to vote 'yes' to pass, as per Section 206(1) of the Vancouver Charter.
agenda_briefs.action_required: If you run a community sport event in Vancouver, watch for the council decision on April 14, 2026 — this could mean grant funding for your Spring 2026 event.

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: Burnaby property tax distribution for 2026
agenda_briefs.summary: Council is being asked to approve a 2026 property tax distribution that keeps the tax share at approximately 57.5% residential and 42.5% non-residential, with no tax shift. The total general purposes tax levy is $1,224,250,000. Tax rates will be adjusted for assessment market changes to ensure revenue neutrality. The recommendation also continues the blended tax rate for Light industry (Class 5) and Business/other (Class 6) properties, and extends targeted 5-year land assessment averaging for Residential (Class 1), Light industry (Class 5), and Business/other (Class 6) properties.
agenda_briefs.action_required: Watch for the council decision on April 14, 2026 — it will determine your 2026 property tax rates.

key_dates.label: Council vote on 2026 property tax distribution

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: Burnaby 2025 property tax exemptions for non-profits
agenda_briefs.summary: Council is set to vote on a report that recommends granting property tax exemptions for 2025 to certain non-profit organizations in Burnaby. A property tax exemption means the organization does not have to pay property tax on the land or building it owns or leases. This decision would affect which groups get the break and how much tax revenue the city gives up. The report is on the agenda for an upcoming council meeting and has not been approved yet.
agenda_briefs.action_required: No action required from you at this time. Watch for the council decision to see if your property or business is affected.

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: Burnaby Coastal Flood Management Policy
agenda_briefs.summary: Council is set to vote on a proposed Coastal Flood Management Policy that clarifies the City's role in coastal flood management. The policy formalizes existing practices and emphasizes leveraging land-use authority and redevelopment opportunities to improve long-term flood resilience. City-built structural measures outside redevelopment will be considered only in specific situations to preserve City facilities and core services, subject to financial capacity.
agenda_briefs.action_required: Check if your property is in a proposed flood-risk zone and review the draft policy before the council vote.

agenda_briefs.summary: Council will vote on approving the Vancouver Heritage Foundation's 2025 Annual Report and a $152,375 grant from the 2026 Operating Budget for the first year of a three-year operating agreement (2026-2028). The grant requires at least two-thirds of all council members to vote in favour under Section 206(1) of the Vancouver Charter.

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: Burnaby council to vote on public plaza alcohol sites for 2026-2029
agenda_briefs.summary: Council will vote on a proposal to designate 13 public plazas for alcohol consumption from 2026 to 2029, plus a 2-year extension for the Drinker's Lounge parklet at 111 Princess St. The program would transition to a 3-year cycle. Permissible hours align with nearby washroom operating hours. All proposed sites have washroom access via portable units or nearby facilities.
agenda_briefs.action_required: Watch for the April 14, 2026 council meeting and contact your city councillor if you have concerns about a plaza near your business.

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: Burnaby council to apply for development approvals funding
agenda_briefs.summary: Council will vote on endorsing a $200,000 grant application to the Union of BC Municipalities Local Government Development Approvals Program for the Vancouver Housing Service Redesign project. The grant would fund a cross-department initiative to streamline approvals for large-scale housing developments by simplifying regulations, creating reusable guides and templates, and triaging applications by complexity. The application was submitted March 13, 2026, and requires a Council resolution before a contribution agreement can be signed.
agenda_briefs.action_required: Watch for the council vote — if approved, this could speed up your future development applications in Vancouver.

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: Burnaby council to vote on housing rezoning rule changes
agenda_briefs.summary: Council will vote on amendments to housing rezoning conditions for 11 specific developments, primarily under the Rental Development Relief Program (RDRP). The RDRP is a two-year pilot (February 1, 2026 – December 15, 2027) for eligible mid- and high-rise BMR projects. Eligible projects may adjust BMR affordability levels from a 20% discount to CMHC city-wide average rents, to rates not exceeding city-wide averages for all purpose-built rental apartments (up to a 25% discount compared to 2024 market rents).
agenda_briefs.action_required: If you own or are developing one of the 11 listed properties, review the proposed condition changes and prepare to negotiate amended Housing Agreements with the City.

key_dates.label: Rental Development Relief Program pilot ends

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: Text amendment to CD-1 (839) bylaw for 5590 Victoria Drive and 2005 East 40th Avenue
agenda_briefs.summary: Council is set to vote on a proposed text amendment to the Comprehensive Development (CD-1) Bylaw No. 13493 for properties at 5590 Victoria Drive and 2005 East 40th Avenue. The amendment would change the zoning from for-profit affordable rental housing (leased by a non-profit, with 30% of units for households below HILs and rent capped at 30% of income) to 100% secured market rental housing. The floor space ratio (FSR) would increase from 3.49 to 3.5. The maximum building height is 22.3 m. At least 35% of units must have two or more bedrooms.
agenda_briefs.action_required: Watch for the council decision date — this amendment could affect development rules for these properties.

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: CD-1 rezoning at 4088 Granville Street, Burnaby
agenda_briefs.summary: Burnaby City Council is set to vote on a proposed bylaw that would rezone the property at 4088 Granville Street from FSD (First Shaughnessy) District to CD-1 (Comprehensive Development) District. The rezoning would increase the maximum floor space ratio from 0.5 to 1.2 and the maximum building height from 11.5 m (38 ft.) to 13.4 m (44 ft.) to permit three three-storey residential rental buildings containing 31 units. At least 35% of units must have 2+ bedrooms. This is a first reading of the draft bylaw — it has not been approved yet and will require further council votes before it can take effect.
agenda_briefs.action_required: If you own property or a business near 4088 Granville Street, watch for the public hearing date to have your say on this proposed rezoning.

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: Rezoning for 3803-3823 West 10th Ave and 2553 Highbury St
agenda_briefs.summary: Council is set to vote on a proposed rezoning for properties at 3803-3823 West 10th Avenue and 2553 Highbury Street. If approved, the project would change the land-use rules (CD-1 rezoning) to allow a six-storey mixed-use building with 82 secured market rental units and commercial at grade. The proposal includes a time-limited condition: if a Stage 1 building permit is not issued within two years of approval, at least 20% of residential floor area must revert to below-market rental units. The maximum floor space ratio is 3.50, building height is 22 m, and minimum commercial floor area is 594.9 m². The sign bylaw and noise control bylaw would also be amended.

agenda_briefs.summary: Council is set to vote on a proposed bylaw that would rezone 310 East 14th Avenue from R3-3 to CD-1, allowing a 20-storey residential building with 138 rental units. 20% of the residential floor area must be secured as below-market rental units. agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: Burnaby bylaw to assess property for Trounce Alley and Blood Alley maintenance
agenda_briefs.summary: Council is set to vote on a proposed bylaw that would charge property owners in the Trounce Alley and Blood Alley Square area for the 2025 maintenance costs of those public spaces. A 'local improvement project' is a way for a city to pay for upgrades or upkeep in a specific area by billing the properties that benefit from the work. If approved, this bylaw would mean owners of affected properties would have to pay a share of the maintenance costs, likely through a special charge on their property tax bill.
agenda_briefs.action_required: Check if your property is in the Trounce Alley or Blood Alley Square area and watch for the council vote.

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: Burnaby bylaw to assess property costs for East Hastings parking project
agenda_briefs.summary: Council is set to vote on a proposed bylaw that would charge certain property owners in the East Hastings area for the costs of a shared parking project in 2025. The bylaw would 'assess real property' — meaning it would add a special fee or tax to specific properties to pay for the collective parking project. This is not yet approved; it is up for discussion and a vote at an upcoming council meeting.
agenda_briefs.action_required: Watch for the council meeting date and consider attending or submitting comments if your property is in the East Hastings area.

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: Housing agreement for 320-360 West 2nd Avenue up for council vote
agenda_briefs.summary: Burnaby City Council is set to vote on a proposed bylaw that would create a Housing Agreement for the property at 320-360 West 2nd Avenue. A Housing Agreement is a legal contract between the city and a property owner that locks in how the building will be used — in this case, to ensure the units remain as affordable rental housing for a set number of years. This is a proposed decision only; it has not been approved yet.

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: Amend housing agreement for 450-496 Prior Street, Burnaby
agenda_briefs.summary: Council is set to vote on a proposed bylaw that would change an existing Housing Agreement for properties at 450-496 Prior Street, 550 Malkin Avenue, and 1002 Station Street. A Housing Agreement is a legal contract between the city and a property owner that sets rules about how the housing is used — for example, keeping some units affordable. This amendment would update the terms of that agreement. The decision has not been made yet.

minutes_briefs.title: Council approved $2.8M in institutional, $710K annual, and $5.7M multi-year cultural grants
minutes_briefs.summary: Burnaby City Council approved a package of 2026 cultural grants totaling over $9.2 million. This includes $2,828,160 for five major institutions, $709,760 for 26 annual grant recipients (plus an extra $25,000 for the Dr. Sun Yat-Sen Garden Society), and $1,897,550 per year for three years (2026–2028) for 52 multi-year grant recipients. Council also approved a $175,000 grant (with amended conditions) and an additional $75,000 to the Vancouver Concert Hall and Theatre Society for a feasibility study, plus a one-time $728,000 grant to Historic Joy Kogawa House Society for hazardous materials removal and accessibility upgrades. The grants are now final and funding will be distributed according to the approved terms.
minutes_briefs.action_required: If your organization is listed in the approved grant schedule, contact the City's Arts, Culture, and Community Services department to finalize your grant agreement and arrange disbursement.
minutes_briefs.source_body:
2. 2026 Cultural Grants (Balance of Operating, Cultural Spaces) March 30, 2026

Staff from Arts, Culture and Community Services responded to questions.

Council heard from two speakers who spoke in opposition to the report recommendations.

D. THAT Council approve amended conditions as further described on page 6 under the section titled “Recommendations D to F: Making Space for Arts and Culture Grant” of the Report dated March 30, 2026, entitled “2026 Cultural Grants (Balance of Operating, Cultural Spaces),” for the disbursement of the Making Space for Arts and Culture grant of $175,000 approved by Council on December 10, 2025, (Council Report RTS 18091) to the Vancouver Concert Hall and Theatre Society to support development of Phase II of their Cultural Precinct Feasibility Study.

amended

minutes_briefs.title: Council approved 19 Vancouver community sport event grants totalling $89,000
minutes_briefs.summary: Vancouver City Council approved 19 new Community Sport Event Grants worth a total of $89,000 for the Spring 2026 intake. The grants go to organizations listed in the report to help them host community sport events. Each grant recipient must agree to the program's terms and conditions and sign a grant agreement letter before any money is paid out. Council also deemed any non-charity organizations listed in the report as contributing to the city's culture, beautification, health, or welfare, which is a legal step required under the Vancouver Charter to allow them to receive the grant.
minutes_briefs.source_body:
3. Vancouver Community Sport Event Grants – Spring 2026 Intake March 10, 2026

A. THAT Council approve 19 new Vancouver Community Sport Event Grants totalling $89,000 as outlined in the body of the Report dated March 10, 2026, entitled “Vancouver Community Sport Event Grants – Spring 2026 Intake,” to each organization listed in Table 1 of the same report, under the column entitled “Organization” and for the amount set out beside their name in the column entitled “Grant Recommended”; source of funding is the 2026 Vancouver Community Sport Hosting Program Budget.

B. THAT the grants in A above be subject to each grant recipient agreeing to the terms and conditions of the community sport hosting grant program as generally outlined in this report and otherwise satisfactory to the Director, Tourism and Destination Events and the City Solicitor.

C. THAT no legal rights or obligations are created by the approval of the A and B above unless and until the grant agreement letter is executed and delivered by the grant recipient.

D. THAT pursuant to the Section 206(1)(i) of the Vancouver Charter, the organizations listed in Table 1 of the Report dated March 10, 2026, entitled “Vancouver Community Sport Event Grants – Spring 2026 Intake,” which are not otherwise a registered charity with Canada Revenue Agency are deemed by Council to be an organization contributing to the culture, beautification, health, or welfare of the City.

ADOPTED ON CONSENT AND A BY THE REQUIRED MAJORITY (Vote No. 11523) (Councillor Kirby-Yung absent for the vote)

minutes_briefs.title: Vancouver Council adopted Coastal Flood Management Policy to guide flood planning
minutes_briefs.summary: Vancouver City Council has adopted the Coastal Flood Management Policy, which outlines the City's role in planning and managing both structural (like seawalls) and non-structural (like land-use rules) measures to protect against coastal flooding and sea level rise. A proposed amendment to create a multi-government task force for the False Creek floodplain was defeated. The policy was approved unanimously by Council.
minutes_briefs.industries: Construction & Development, Landlord & Property Mgmt, Transportation & Logistics
minutes_briefs.source_body:
6. Coastal Flood Management Policy March 17, 2026

Staff from Planning, Urban Design and Sustainability and Legal Services responded to questions.

MOVED by Councillor Maloney SECONDED by Councillor Meiszner

THAT Council adopt the Coastal Flood Management Policy, generally as presented in Appendix A of the Report dated March 17, 2026, entitled “Coastal Flood Management Policy,” which articulates the City’s role and supports planning, implementation, and management of structural and non-structural coastal flood management measures in Vancouver.

carried

AMENDMENT MOVED by Councillor Maloney SECONDED by Councillor Orr

A. THAT Council adopt the Coastal Flood Management Policy, generally as presented in Appendix A to the Report dated March 17, 2026, entitled “Coastal Flood Management Policy.”

B. THAT Council directs staff to report back on the scope of work, governance options, resource requirements, and timeline necessary for the City to take the steps necessary to convene, or cause to be convened, an intergovernmental and inter-agency task force for the False Creek floodplain, for the purpose of:

1. identifying major publicly owned, utility, transportation, health, and civic assets at risk from coastal flooding and sea level rise;

2. assessing whether a coordinated, area-wide structural flood management approach would be more effective and cost-efficient than fragmented site-by-site measures;

3. identifying the appropriate roles of the City, Province, Government of Canada, First Nations, agencies, utilities, and other major landowners and infrastructure owners, including, but not limited to, Canada Mortgage and Housing Corporation in respect of Granville Island, the Department of National Defence and/or the Canadian Coast Guard in respect of the Kitsilano Coast Guard Station, Fisheries and Oceans Canada in respect of the False Creek Harbour Authority, PAVCo in respect of B.C. Place, Providence Health Care in respect to St. Paul's Hospital, Vancouver Fraser Port Authority, Vancouver Airport Authority, TransLink, BC Hydro, Creative Energy, and such other affected public or regulated entities as staff may determine;

4. identifying advocacy, research, technical, and partnership work required to support any future funding strategy or senior-government engagement; and

5. reporting back on options for a fiscally prudent convening and coordination role for the City that does not prejudice future Council decisions on capital commitments.

C. THAT the report back in B above also assesses whether similar multi-jurisdictional coordination challenges exist in the Burrard Inlet and Fraser River floodplains, or whether differences in land ownership, infrastructure ownership, and distribution of benefits mean that a False Creek-specific approach is warranted.

LOST (Vote No. 11516) (Councillors Dominato, Klassen, Meiszner, Montague, Zhou and Mayor Sim opposed) (Councillors Bligh and Kirby-Yung absent for the vote)

The amendment having lost, the motion was put and CARRIED UNANIMOUSLY (Vote No. 11517) with Councillors Bligh and Kirby-Yung absent for the vote.

minutes_briefs.title: Council approved in principle CD-1 text amendment at 5590 Victoria Drive
minutes_briefs.summary: Burnaby Council voted to approve in principle a text amendment to the CD-1 (Comprehensive Development) bylaw for 5590 Victoria Drive and 2005 East 40th Avenue. The amendment increases the floor space ratio (FSR) from 3.49 to 3.5 and changes the housing requirement from for-profit affordable rental housing (leased by a non-profit, with 30% of units for low-income households) to secured market rental housing. The approval is subject to conditions, including a Housing Agreement bylaw to be prepared before the final bylaw is enacted.

(ii) THAT any approval that may be granted shall not obligate the City to enact a by-law rezoning the property, and any costs incurred in fulfilling requirements imposed as a condition of rezoning are at the risk of the property owner; and

(iii) THAT the City and all its officials, including the Approving Officer, shall not in any way be limited or directed in the exercise of their authority or discretion, regardless of when they are called upon to exercise such authority or discretion.

CARRIED (Vote No. 11520) (Councillor Orr opposed) (Councillors Bligh and Kirby-Yung absent for the vote)

minutes_briefs.title: Council approved rezoning for 31 rental units at 4088 Granville Street
minutes_briefs.summary: Burnaby City Council approved in principle a rezoning application for 4088 Granville Street. The property will be rezoned from FSD (First Shaughnessy District) to CD-1 (Comprehensive Development District), which is a special zone that allows more flexibility in building design. This change increases the maximum floor space ratio (the amount of floor area allowed compared to the lot size) from 0.5 to 1.2, and raises the maximum building height from 11.5 metres (38 feet) to 13.4 metres (44 feet). The approval allows the owner to build three three-storey residential rental buildings with 31 units. The approval is subject to conditions, including a Housing Agreement that must be finalized before the new zoning bylaw can take effect.
minutes_briefs.action_required: If you are the property owner or developer, review the conditions in Appendix B of the March 10, 2026 report and prepare to meet them before the CD-1 bylaw is enacted.

minutes_briefs.title: Council approved in principle a rezoning for a six-storey mixed-use building on West 10th Avenue
minutes_briefs.summary: Burnaby City Council voted unanimously to approve in principle a rezoning application for 3803-3823 West 10th Avenue and 2553 Highbury Street. The property will be rezoned from RR-3B (Residential Rental) to CD-1 (Comprehensive Development) District, allowing a six-storey mixed-use building with commercial space on the ground floor and 82 secured market rental housing units. If a stage one Building Permit is not issued within two years of this approval, the developer must include below-market rental units. Council also approved the draft CD-1 bylaw, the form of development, and instructed staff to prepare a Housing Agreement Bylaw and amendments to the Sign Bylaw and Noise Control Bylaw. The approvals are conditional and create no legal rights or obligations for the City until final bylaws are enacted.
minutes_briefs.action_required: No immediate action required — the rezoning is approved in principle; final bylaws and permits will follow.
minutes_briefs.source_body:
13. CD-1 Rezoning: 3803-3823 West 10th Avenue and 2553 Highbury Street March 10, 2026

* * * * *

At this point in the meeting, it was

MOVED by Councillor Montague SECONDED by Councillor Zhou

THAT Council waive the staff presentation for Report 13 – CD-1 Rezoning: 3803-3823 West 10th Avenue and 2553 Highbury Street.

CARRIED UNANIMOUSLY (Councillors Bligh and Kirby-Yung absent for the vote)

* * * * *

MOVED by Councillor Dominato SECONDED by Councillor Klassen

A. THAT the application by Sightline Properties (Highbury Street) Ltd., the registered owner of the lands located at 3803-3823 West 10th Avenue and 2553 Highbury Street [PID 032-300-492; Lot A Block 183 District Lot 176 Group 1 New Westminster District Plan EPP138582], to rezone the lands from RR-3B (Residential Rental) District to CD-1 (Comprehensive Development) District, to permit a six storey mixed-use building containing commercial at grade and 82 secured market rental housing units, including below-market units if a stage one Building Permit for this development has not been issued within two (2) years of this approval, be approved in principle;

FURTHER THAT the draft CD-1 By-law, in accordance with Appendix A of the Report dated March 10, 2026, entitled “CD-1 Rezoning: 3803-3823 West 10th Avenue and 2553 Highbury Street,” be approved in principle;

FURTHER THAT the proposed form of development also be approved in principle, generally as prepared by GBL Architects Inc., received April 3, 2025 and supplementary drawings received on January 6, 2026;

FURTHER THAT the above approvals be subject to the Conditions of Approval contained in Appendix B of the above-noted report;

AND FURTHER THAT subject to the approval in principle of the rezoning and any subsequent enactment of the CD-1 By-law, if a stage one Building Permit for the development has not been issued within two (2) years of the approval in principle of the rezoning, the Director of Legal Services be instructed to bring forward the amendment to the CD-1 By-law, generally as set out in Appendix D of the above-noted report.

B. THAT subject to approval in principle of the rezoning and the Housing Agreement described in Part 2 of Appendix B of the Report dated March 10, 2026, entitled “CD-1 Rezoning: 3803-3823 West 10th Avenue and 2553 Highbury Street,” the Director of Legal Services be instructed to prepare the necessary Housing Agreement By-law for enactment prior to enactment of the CD-1 By-law, subject to such terms and conditions as may be required at the discretion of the Director of Legal Services and the General Manager of Planning, Urban Design and Sustainability.

C. THAT subject to approval of the CD-1 By-laws, the application to amend the Sign By law to establish regulations for each of the CD-1s, generally as set out in Appendix C of the Report dated March 10, 2026, entitled “CD-1 Rezoning: 3803-3823 West 10th Avenue and 2553 Highbury Street,” be approved.

D. THAT subject to approval of the CD-1 By-law, the Noise Control By-law be amended to include the CD-1, generally set out in Appendix C of the Report dated March 10, 2026, entitled “CD-1 Rezoning: 3803-3823 West 10th Avenue and 2553 Highbury Street”;

FURTHER THAT the Director of Legal Services be instructed to bring forward the amendment to the Noise Control By-law at the time of enactment of the CD-1 By-law.

E. THAT A to D above be adopted on the following conditions:

(i) THAT the passage of the above resolutions creates no legal rights for the applicant or any other person, or obligation on the part of the City and any expenditure of funds or incurring of costs is at the risk of the person making the expenditure or incurring the cost;

(ii) THAT any approval that may be granted following the Council meeting shall not obligate the City to enact a by-law rezoning the property, and any costs incurred in fulfilling requirements imposed as a condition of rezoning are at the risk of the property owner; and

(iii) THAT the City and all its officials, including the Approving Officer, shall not in any way be limited or directed in the exercise of their authority or discretion, regardless of when they are called upon to exercise such authority or discretion.

CARRIED UNANIMOUSLY (Vote No. 11521) (Councillor Orr abstained from the vote) (Councillors Bligh and Kirby-Yung absent for the vote)

minutes_briefs.title: Council approved in principle rezoning for 29-storey tower at 888-896 Cambie
minutes_briefs.summary: Burnaby City Council voted to approve in principle a rezoning application for 888-896 Cambie Street and 111-191 Smithe Street. The decision allows the developer, Nonni Property Group, to rezone the land from Downtown (DD) to Comprehensive Development (CD-1) district. This will increase the maximum floor space ratio from 5.0 to 19.5, permitting a 29-storey mixed-use building with 165 rental housing units, 246 hotel rooms, and ground-floor commercial space. Council also approved in principle the heritage designation of the Cambie Street facade of the existing Stanley Brock Building. The rezoning and heritage bylaw will now go to a Public Hearing before final adoption.

minutes_briefs.title: Council approved in principle CD-1 rezoning for 16-storey hotel at 888 West 8th Avenue
minutes_briefs.summary: Burnaby City Council voted unanimously to approve in principle a rezoning application for 888 West 8th Avenue. The property will be rezoned from C-3A (Commercial) to CD-1 (Comprehensive Development) District, allowing a 16-storey commercial building with 152 hotel rooms and ground-floor retail. The maximum floor space ratio (FSR) — the ratio of a building's total floor area to the size of the land it sits on — increases from 3.0 to 8.1, and the maximum building height rises from 9.2 metres (30 feet) to 50.6 metres (166 feet). The approval is subject to conditions in the staff report, and the City's legal team will prepare the final zoning bylaw for adoption.
minutes_briefs.action_required: If you own or operate a business near 888 West 8th Avenue, review the new CD-1 zoning conditions once the bylaw is enacted to understand how the hotel and commercial development may affect traffic, parking, or local competition.

minutes_briefs.title: Council approved new C-2A zone to fast-track rental housing and small hotels
minutes_briefs.summary: Burnaby City Council approved a new mixed-use commercial district called C-2A. This new zoning category is designed to speed up approvals for rental housing and small hotels. Council voted to rezone certain properties from existing commercial zones (C-2, C-2B, C-2C, and C-2C1) to the new C-2A district. The decision also updates several related bylaws — including the Rental Housing Stock Official Development Plan, Subdivision By-law, Sign By-law, Noise Control By-law, Parking By-law, and the Downtown Eastside/Oppenheimer Official Development Plan — to make them consistent with the new C-2A rules. The bylaw amendments will be prepared for enactment.
minutes_briefs.action_required: Check if your property is among those being rezoned to the new C-2A district and review the updated rules for rental housing and small hotel development.
minutes_briefs.industries: Construction & Development, Hotel & Accommodation, Landlord & Property Mgmt

minutes_briefs.title: Council approved 57.5% residential, 42.5% non-residential property tax split for 2026
minutes_briefs.summary: Burnaby City Council voted to set the 2026 property tax share at approximately 57.5% for residential properties and 42.5% for non-residential properties (including commercial, industrial, and business properties). The Director of Finance has been instructed to calculate the 2026 general purpose tax rates for all property classes to achieve this split. This decision is final and was adopted on consent at the April 7, 2026 council meeting.
minutes_briefs.action_required: Review your 2026 property tax bill when issued to understand how this tax share split affects your specific property class and tax rate.

minutes_briefs.title: Burnaby Council adopted 2026 budget with $1.224B property tax levy
minutes_briefs.summary: Burnaby City Council approved the 2026 budget, which includes a $1.224 billion property tax levy. This means the city will collect that amount from property taxes to help pay for city services, staff, and other expenses. The total city spending for 2026 is set at $2.39 billion. The budget was adopted by council on consent, meaning it passed without debate, with one councillor absent.
